coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in synthesis and biological applications of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Indian Chemical Society
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Karmakar, Sudip
Das, Tushar Kanti
Kundu, Somashree
Maiti, Susmita
Saha, Abhijit
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are becoming imperative functional carbon-based nanomaterials for use in a wide range of biological applications due to the unique optical and optoelectronic properties and also for having physical and chemical stable carbon network structure. Low toxicity, edge functionalization, tunable size, and photoluminescence properties of GQDs have attracted worldwide interests in recent years from academic and industrial point of view. The strong photoluminescence, good water solubility, and high drug loading capacity make GQDs useful for biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. In this review, we have focused on the recent development in the synthesis methodologies and biological applications of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Biosensing
Bioimaging
Drug delivery
Biocompatibility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of synthesis of GQDs via high temperature high pressure (HTHP) hydrothermal method using GO and H2O2 as starting material. Reproduced with permission [76]. Copyright 2018, American Chemical Society. (b) Synthesis of GQDs from spherical carbon black by heating in HNO3 through acidic exfoliation method. Reproduced with permission [88]. Copyright 2015, Royal Society of Chemistry.(c) Preparation of GQDs via electrochemical exfoliation of carbon fibers in pure ionic liquid electrolyte. Reproduced with permission [89]. Copyright 2017, Royal Society of Chemistry.

(a) A scheme for the preparation of folic acid functionalized GQDs through pyrolysis of citric acid. Reproduced with permission [103] Copyright 2017, American Chemical Society. (b) synthesis of GQDs assemblies through microwave assisted one-pot reaction. Reproduced with permission [114] Copyright 2018, Royal Society of Chemistry.

(a) Schematic illustration of in-vivo monitoring of GQDs-based composite for drug delivery, and response in cancer cell. Reproduced with permission [120]. Copyright 2017, American Chemical Society. (b) Synthesis of PEGMA@GQDs@γ-CD-MOF composite and loading of DOX (B) pH-responsive controlled drug release. Reproduced with permission [123]. Copyright 2019, Royal Society of Chemistry.

Bright field images of a) Control HeLa cells, b) cellular uptake of Nd-GQDs c) Tm-GQDs after incubation period of 6 ​h in cells; d) Change in the fluorescence intensity of Nd-GQDs e) Tm-GQDs in HeLa cells over 24 ​h time period. Reproduced with permission [130] Copyright 2020, Wiley-VCH GmbH.

a) Bright-field images of yolk of embryos after introduction of IAPP into it and toxicity mitigation by pre-incubation of GQDs. b) Graphical presentation of percentage hatching, malformation and mortality of embryos after microinjection of different amount of GQDs along with IAPP. c) ROS generation upon microinjecting GQDs and IAPP characterized by DCF fluorecsnece. Reproduced with permission [135]. Copyright 2018, Royal Society of Chemistry.

(a) Schematic representation of mechanism of FRET based sensing of small cell lung cancer detection. Reproduced with permission [140]. Copyright 2020, American Chemical Society.(b) Schematic diagram on aggregation state change of GQDs-aptamer based fluorescent signal-on based platform for the detection of OTA. Reproduced with permission [143]. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in synthesis and biological applications of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata, 700 106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata, 700 106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are becoming imperative functional carbon-based nanomaterials for use in a wide range of biological applications due to the unique optical and optoelectronic properties and also for having physical and chemical stable carbon network structure. Low toxicity, edge functionalization, tunable size, and photoluminescence properties of GQDs have attracted worldwide interests in recent years from academic and industrial point of view. The strong photoluminescence, good water solubility, and high drug loading capacity make GQDs useful for biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. In this review, we have focused on the recent development in the synthesis methodologies and biological applications of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single layer of graphite arranged in 2D honeycomb lattice, has been extensively studied by researchers for the past few years [1]. Graphene exhibits many interesting properties like good electrical conductivity, ultra-light weight, flexibility, large Böhr exciton diameter, etc. [2–5] However, there are some limitations (no bandgap, susceptive to oxidative atmosphere) that hinder it to be applied in different scientific research areas [6]. The modification in the structure of graphene is one of the important aspects to make it available for potential use. The discovery of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by Ponomarenko et al. [7] have overcome the limitations exhibited by graphene.

Over the last two decades, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) have dominated the field of nanoscience and nanotechnology owing to unique and well characterized optical properties. SQDs like CdSe, ZnSe, ZnTe, CdS, CdTe, ZnS, PbSe, etc. stabilized by some suitable capping ligands, show size dependent optical properties having broad excitation with narrow emission. High luminescence quantum yield and symmetric emission exhibited by SQDs made them promising candidate for the applications related to sensing of metal ions and biomolecules, in-vitro bioimaging, and immunosensing assays [8–14]. Furthermore, large surface area to volume ratio of SQDs can be utilized for the attachment of multiple biomolecules to its surface, in order to produce desired composite for application in the biomedical field [15]. However, presence of heavy metal atom in SQDs resulting in toxicity has limited its suitability in in - vivo imaging, cancer treatment, drug delivery [16]. In addition, the elements used for SQDs are sometimes expensive as well. Thus, in recent years, GQDs have provided an alternative non-toxic candidate which can be synthesized from abundant carbon sources available in nature.

Zero-dimensional (0D) GQDs, the latest carbon family member, contains single or few layers of graphene with different functionalized groups on its edge [17,18] with a size less than 20 ​nm [19]. GQDs show several interesting characteristics, such as stable photoluminescence, biocompatibility, high PL quantum yield, excellent solubility, surface grafting and so on [20,21]. Unique properties illustrate tremendous potential of GQDs for applications in the diverse field of modern scientific research and technology. The applications of GQDs in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, biosensor, drug delivery, and other biomedical fields are being extensively investigated [22–25].

Researchers are developing several synthesis procedures to fabricate GQDs with modified characteristics, such as enhanced PLQY, well-defined edge termination, and enhanced photostability [26,27]. The modification in GQDs have been done in terms of functionalizing with different active groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, aldehyde, amide,etc. [28,29] Introducing dopants like Sulfur, Nitrogen, Phosphorus, etc. in GQDs also improves its physical and chemical properties [30–32]. The emission maximum of GQDs is observed around 420-450 ​nm [33]. However, functionalized or heteroatom doped GQDs show a wide emission window [34,35]. Possibility of tuning PL emission through altering size makes GQDs as promising candidate in biomedical and biological applications [36–39].

GQDs can be synthesized using two synthetic approaches: i) top-down and ii) bottom-up. In the top-down approach, the bulk material is split into nano-sized particles. This approach include cutting bulk materials, like fullerenes, graphene, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, etc. to form GQDs through some methods like hydrothermal, solvothermal, ball milling, and so on. The bottom-up approach fabrication of GQD is carried out through carbonization or controllable chemical reaction of different materials like citric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, etc. The top-down approach is extensively utilized to synthesize GQDs due to the simplicity of this approach. The former method involves multiple and complex steps and in some cases, the reaction goes uncontrollable. There are plenty of reports on these two approaches for synthesizing GQDs. The recent advancement in the synthesis procedure has been highlighted here.

Unique properties of GQDs, have been widely utilized in different fields, especially in the biological applications. Due to low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and water solubility, GQDs have emerged as one of the potential quantum dots compared to the other conventional quantum dots [40,41]. The tunable size and PL emission of GQDs are advantageous in biosensing and bioimaging. These properties can be utilized to produce PL emission at the desired wavelength. The presence of different functional groups on the edge of GQDs enables them to bind effectively with drug molecules. Besides, the available large surface area enhances its binding capacity with other molecules through π-stacking. Moreover, the excellent spectroscopic properties of GQDs can be utilized to sense different biomolecules and metal ions, which bring possibilities of diverse biological applications. Though GQDs are in the spotlight due to all the properties as discussed above, it has some limitations that need to be addressed. Applications of GQDs in different fields like electrochemical, optoelectronic, energy-related field, etc. have been reviewed by different research groups [42–46]. In the present article, we have reviewed the latest progress on the fabrication of GQDs and their potential applications in the biological field. Limitations and future possibilities also have been discussed.

GQD, a quantum dot with lateral dimension of sub-100 nm shows quantum confinement in all three dimensions. It has a distinguishable crystalline structure among other carbon quantum dots (CQDs) having similar PL emission [47]. The available functional groups at the edge of GQDs alters the bandgap. Therefore, optical properties of GQDs can be tuned by introducing different functional groups at the edge. The presence of functional groups at the edge of GQDs also help recombination of the electron-hole pairs near the edge which is responsible for the source of PL emission. Besides, defects in the GQDs can be created by doping with suitable materials on it. It has been found that doping in the GQDs with heteroatoms like N, P, S, or F enhances the PLQY.

Graphene has zero energy bandgap, which enables it to conduct electricity. However, defects on the surface of graphene could alter the band gap by either introducing oxygen-containing functional groups or by doping heteroatoms in it. Doping with different heteroatom results in opening of band gap in graphene [48]. Both density functional and time–dependent density functional theory based calculations reveal that doping with heteroatoms can alter the electronic and optical properties of GQDs [49]. The electronic structure of GQDs can be tuned by introducing an inorganic neutral cluster of ions [50]. The work mainly presented the non-covalent modification of GQDs. The theoretical study helped understand the electronic structure of GQDs. It has been found that clusterized ions can significantly polarize the electron energy levels of GQDs. The ion and the counter-ion positions can influence the polarising action.

There is no involvement of d- or f-orbital in GQDs. However, the origin of magnetism in GQDs has attracted wider interest among the different research groups. The magnetism was assumed to be especially intriguing [51]. GQDs have a high edge-to-area ratio and also a possible substantial spin-polarized edge state at the zigzag segments. From the theoretical calculations, it is predicted that the spin-polarized edge states may attribute to the origin of magnetism in GQDs. It has been reported that GQDs exhibit diamagnetic and paramagnetic behavior at low temperatures. The geometric shape, defects, and reconstruction at the edge influence the magnetic behavior of GQDs [52]. Furthermore, the shape irregularity and edge roughness govern spin-polarized edge state strongly.

GQDs show absorption in the visible range of 260–330 ​nm due to the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds. Based on the different dopant, surface functionalizing groups, size, and synthesis procedure, the emission wavelength of GQDs may differ [34,53]. There are reports on ultra-violet [54,55], blue [56–58], green [36,59], yellow [60,61] and red [62,63] emitting GQDs. Generally, blue and green-emitting GQDs are common, whereas reports on red emitting GQDs are limited. Interestingly, Tang et al. [63] demonstrated broadband emission from N doped GQDs (N-GQDs) in the range of 300–1000 ​nm. The large conjugated system created by the layered structure of N-GQDs contains extensive delocalized π-electrons, which are responsible for this broadband emission. Hence, doping with a desired heteroatom would be a key to access the PL emission window shown by GQDs [64]. Besides, depending on the distribution of size of the GQDs in the solution, the emission could be either excitation dependent or independent. Monodispersed GQDs have a higher probability to exhibit excitation independent PL emission [33].

GQDs have widely been used in the biological field due to their excellent biocompatibility. Unlike graphene, GQD is highly soluble in water because of different functional groups present at its edge and surface. Besides, it has low toxicity as it is primarily composed of carbon. Hence unlike the metallic residue, GQDs have a minimal chance to get accumulated in tissue. The presence of different functional groups may contribute to an increase in the toxicity level of GQDs within a tolerance limit [65]. However, GQDs on irradiation with light produced singlet oxygen or reactive oxygen species. This photo-induced oxygen species may trigger cytotoxicity in the cellular environment. The generated singlet oxygen however could be useful if we consider the photodynamic therapy related applications, which has been reviewed recently [66]. The presence of oxygenated functional groups, such as aldehyde, carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and ketones are responsible for the generation of singlet oxygen in the cell environment. The degree of generation of singlet oxygen is dependent on the type of functional group present and the time of exposure to the irradiation. The presence of different functional groups at the edge of GQDs can be advantageous as drugs can be attached with ease. GQDs can be used as an excellent carrier for nano-drug delivery. Several carriers have been reported as a carrier of drug delivery but GQDs dominates over those due to its blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability [67]. GQDs are lipid-soluble with lateral dimension of less than 20 ​nm, which make it a perfect candidate to cross BBB by glucose transport protein. The small size helps GQDs cross BBB easily, which may be useful for targeted drug delivery in the brain.

Top-down and bottom-up are well known synthetic approaches that are used extensively to synthesis GQDs. The top-down approach includes the synthesis of GQDs from the bulk carbon material, such as, graphene, graphene oxide, carbon fiber, or carbon black by cutting to small fragments [68] through hydrothermal or solvothermal reaction, acidic exfoliation, oxidative cleavage, etc. Synthesis of GQDs from small organic molecules through carbonization, pyrolysis, stepwise synthesis methods are included in bottom-up approach. Here we have demonstrated some recently reported methods of synthesis of GQDs using both top-down and bottom-up approaches.

For synthesizing GQD, top-down approach is one of the most utilized strategies. The simple operation and availability of the starting materials are the key factor for this strategy. It also facilitates the presence of functional groups at the edge of GQDs. Recent progress on the synthesis of GQDs through top-down strategy is delineated below.

Synthesis of GQDs through hydrothermal method is common and relatively simpler [69–71]. GQDs were first synthesized using the hydrothermal approach in 2010 by cutting graphene sheets [72]. Later the synthesis method was further improved [73]. Recently, there are several reports on synthesis of GQDs using GO or graphene powder [74–77] as starting material. Halder et al. [76] synthesized GQDs employing hydrothermal method at high temperature and high pressure. The available epoxy groups on GO reacted with the .OH radical, produced from H2O2 at high temperature and high pressure, to from GQDs, which has been presented in Fig. 1 a. Basically in this method, high temperature and pressure are applied to break the bulk materials into small fragments [78]. Recently, Luo et al. [79] successfully synthesized GQDs from GO in 4 ​h by employing hydrothermal methods. Zhang et al. [80] reported a top-down hydrothermal strategy to synthesize GQDs with a uniform size of less than 5 ​nm. They used GO as the starting material, which was synthesized by following the modified Hummers' method. Nitric acid which is a strong oxidizing agent was used to convert GO into GQDs through sonication and hydrothermal process. The as-synthesized GQDs effectively restrain the restack of graphene nanosheets due to their uniform nanoscale size of less than 5 ​nm. Edge effect due to the nanoscale size of GQDs helps create more active sites that can promote ion diffusion in the interior of the material. Fan et al. [81]reported facile and large-scale preparation method of GQDs-PEI using 1,3,5-trinitropyrene and PEI (Polyethyleneimine) as starting material. GQDs lack targeted selectivity that restricts their applications in biomedicine. To overcome this, PEI was conjugated with GQDs. 40 ​mL 1,3,5-trinitropyrene (5 ​mg/mL) was mixed with 0.1 ​g PEI and subsequently, the mixture was heated at 200 ​°C in an autoclave for 10 ​h to get GQDs-PEI as the final product. Wu et al. [82] synthesized GQDs from nano-sized graphene oxide (NGO). They used the modified Hummers’ method to prepare NGO from graphite flakes. Later, hydrothermal treatment of NGO in ammonia solution was carried out, which resulted in the formation of PL emission tunable GQDs. The PL emission wavelength of GQDs can be tuned only by varying the concentration of ammonia. Hydrophobic and organelle targetable GQDs were also reported. In addition, hydrothermal top-down approach has been utilized to green synthesis of GQDs using biodegradeble [83] materials. Heteroatom doped GQDs can also be prepared by hydrothermal approach [84]. Though this approach is used widely by researchers, harsh conditions like too high temperature and pressure [85,86] limits its applicability of this method for the synthesis of GQDs.

Synthesis of GQDs from carbon sources like GO, coal, etc. through acid exfoliation and oxidation is one of the popular top-down approaches. GQDs prepared by this method are known to have oxygen-containing functional groups, like –OH, –COOH, etc. at its edge. Therefore, these GQDs are polar in nature and get solubilized in water, DMSO, DMF, etc. Maiti et al. [33] have reported an acidic exfoliation and oxidation method to synthesize GQDs employing GO as the precursor. GO was treated with perchloric acid at 100 ​°C for 24 ​h. Interestingly, in contrast to other reported processes, as-synthesized GQDs exhibited excitation independent PL emission when excited with visible photons. The average size of the GQDs was reported as nearly 5.6 ​nm with a high quantum yield ca. 14%. Chhabra et al. [87] reported synthesis of green-emitting GQDs via acid exfoliation of GO by suspending it in concentrated H2SO4 for 2 ​h. Later, KMnO4 (40 ​wt%) was added to it and the resulting solution was heated at 40 ​°C followed by addition of distilled water. Finally, 30% H2O2 was used to ensure complete consumption of KMnO4. The pH of the resulting solution was adjusted to 4 to get yellow emitting GQDs. High purity GQDs, with multicolor PL emission, was synthesized from carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) [88]. A mixture of carbon black and HNO3 was heated at 135 ​°C for 24 ​h and later at 180 ​°C under argon atmosphere for 10 ​h as schematically shown in Fig. 1b. The size of the as-synthesized GQDs was 2–6 ​nm. They claimed that the purity of the synthesized GQDs is 99.96%.

The acidic exfoliation and oxidation method for the preparation of GQDs is simple and effective. However, different inorganic acids having strong fumes at room temperature are also considered toxic and purification process is hazardous and time-consuming.

The electrochemical exfoliation method has some advantage over other used methods (hydro/solvothermal, acidic oxidation) for the synthesis of GQDs. This top-down approach neither requires maintaining harsh conditions, like high temperature and pressure nor the use of strong acids. Unlike the other used methods, the electrochemical exfoliation method is environmental friendly, cost-effective, and scalable. Materials like graphite, carbon fibers or multi-walled carbon nanotube (CNT) having multilayer of sp2-hybridized carbon are used to prepare GQDs through electrochemical exfoliation by applying a high redox voltage. Yan et al. [89] developed a method from large scale production of GQDs through electrochemical exfoliation using carbon fiber as starting material. Fig. 1c illustrates the synthesis mechanism of the ionic liquid functionalized GQDs from carbon fiber (CF) in the anode through intercalation followed by exfoliation. Ahirwar et al. [90] produced GQDs by electrochemical exfoliation method using two graphite rods as electrode. They used citric acid and alkali hydroxide in water as the electrolyte. The distance between the graphite rods was 25 ​mm. Cyclic voltammetry was performed with a voltage range of ±1 ​V. Then chronoamperometry was performed applying +10 ​V with a sensitivity of 0.1 for 30 ​min. The variation of concentration of NaOH resulted in production of GQDs of different size cluster ranging 1.5–4.5 ​nm. More recently, Kalita and coworkers [91] synthesized GQDs from GO via electrochemical exfoliation method. They experimented with a three-electrode system. Platinum wire, platinum foil, and glassy carbon were used as reference wire, working electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. Propylene carbonate with 3 ​mM LiClO4 was used as electrolyte. GO was oxidized using +1.05 ​V for different time duration (8, 12, 16 ​h). They controlled the size of GQDs by fixing the potential at −1.05 ​V for 3 ​h. The experiment was carried out at room temperature which is a key advantage. Tan et al. [92] synthesized red emitting GQDs by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite in K2S2O8 solution using potential of +0.5 ​V without any chemical modification. The average size of the GQDs was 3 ​nm. They reported, the large sp2 clusters of GQDs are responsible for red PL. Recently, Fu et al. [93] reported synthesis of multicolor nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) by direct exfoliation and oxidation of graphite rods. The working bias voltage was +5 ​V. A mixture of NaOH (0.1 ​M) and semicarbazide (0.1 ​M) in water (200 ​mL) were used as electrolyte solution.

Small molecules, such as citric acid, glucose, l-glutamic acid, etc. are used as the starting material for the preparation of GQDs through bottom-up strategy [94–97]. The bottom-up approach has some advantages over the top-down strategy, like well-controlled size and morphology, less time requires for the completion of the reaction, etc. However, this approach has some disadvantages, such as multistep and complex synthetic procedures, poor functionalization at the edge, etc. Some of the synthesis methods with bottom-up approach have been discussed here.

There are plenty of reports on the synthesis of GQDs through pyrolysis or carbonization method [98–102]. Li et al. [103] synthesized GQDs via pyrolysis method by using citric acid. Later, the GQD was functionalized with folic acid (FA) followed by introduction of IR780 dye on the surface of FA-GQDs. The mechanism has been illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Hong et al. [104] reported a novel procedure for the synthesis of GQDs through pyrolysis of trisodium citrate. Trisodium citrate was directly heated at 200 ​°C for 4 ​min. The change in the color of the powder from white to deep brown indicated the formation of GQDs. The size of as-synthesized GQDs is ca. 1.3 ​nm. However, the quantum yield of the product is as low as 3.6%. Ping et al. [105] produced pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) and d-penicillamine co-functionalized GQDs through two-step thermal pyrolysis. Citric acid was used as the starting material along with the PEHA as a functionalizing precursor. They reported size of synthesized GQDs~3.2 ​nm. The quantum yield of the product was found to be extremely high (90%). Recently, synthesis of high-quality GQDs has been reported by Lee et al. [106] through hydrogen assisted pyrolysis of silicon carbide in a vacuum. Synthesized GQDs exhibited highly ordered crystalline structure. Gu et al. [107] reported the synthesis of functionalized GQDs from citric acid and urea through infrared carbonization pyrolysis (IRC process) under IR irradiation.

Microwave assisted synthesis of GQDs has several advantages over other methods [108–112], which include short reaction time, low impurity, reproducibility, energy saving, high yield, etc. Kumawat et al. [113] reported synthesis of GQDs from grape seed extract through microwave digestion process. The quantum yield of the product was ca. 32%. Those GQDs were self-assembled and termed as sGQDs. Due to the high quantum yield, they utilized these sGQDs for cell proliferation, nucleus imaging, and photoluminescent sensing related applications.

Further, GQDs were synthesized from low cost organic precursors employing one-pot microwave assisted synthesis [114]. Urea and glucose were used as the starting materials along with H3PO4. The obtained mixture was heated at different temperatures (140, 150, and 160 ​°C) under microwave radiation for 10 and 20 ​min. The obtained residue was processed and purified by precipitation. Simultaneous production of GQDs and their spontaneous self-assembly forming porous architectures was reported here. GQDs-assemblies were found to be stable in different organic solvents. The synthetic procedure has been depicted in Fig. 2b. Nguyen et al. [115] reported synthesis of GQDs and NGQDs with improved optical properties and quantum yields. The microwave was used for the pyrolysis of citric acid. The PL intensity of GQDs was found to be strongest when the pyrolysis duration was 1–2 ​min. Recently, Yao and co-workers [96] reported the synthesis of GQDs from glucose via bottom-up microwave-assisted synthesis technique. Glucose and ethylene glycol were used as starting materials. GQDs were synthesized within 9 ​min of pyrolysis. The synthesized GQDs can be stored for more than six months without significant loss of luminescence quantum efficiency.

The application of carbon nanomaterial in the biological area has been one of the leading research interests of researchers since recent past years. In particular, GQDs are one of the carbon nanomaterials which has played an important role in the diverse field of applications. To understand the properties shown by GQDs, extensive research has been carried out using different techniques, like doping, surface and edge functionalization, shape, size controlling, and so on. Important features of synthesized GQDs are high quantum yield, tunable PL emission, good water solubility, resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. These unique properties make GQDs a promising carbonaceous nanomaterial for bioimaging and biosensing. Here, we have highlighted some recent advances in biological applications of GQDs.

GQDs have widely been utilized in targeted drug delivery applications [116–119]. Recently, Ding et al. [120] utilized GQDs as a probe to track drug delivery (doxorubicin) in the cancer cell. The composite DOX-GQDs-Cy5.5 was found to be efficient for drug delivery in both in vivo and in vitro due to their excellent tissue penetration ability (Fig. 3 a). As discussed earlier, GQDs have a huge capacity for drug loading due to the large surface area and the presence of edge functional groups. Recently, Iannazzo et al. [121] have reported GQDs based cell traceable and biocompatible cancer-targeted drug delivery system (GQD, GQD-PEG-BFG(3,3-dimethyl-2-(3-methyl-2,3-dihydrobenzofuran-2-yl)butanoate)), GQD@Pyr(pyrene)-RF (riboflavin), GQD-PEG-BFG@Pyr-RF(Red Line), and Pyr-R). They have chosen benzofuran-2-acetic ester derivatives as an anticancer drug and riboflavin (RF) as targeting ligand. The stable π-π interaction between GQDs and the drug was a key advantage for manipulation. Cytotoxicity of GQD-based nanosystems was investigated in various cancer cell lines, where low toxicity effect was observed in all the cases. Besides, dispensability of the drug in water increased, when conjugated with GQDs. Yue et al. [122] have developed graphene quantum dots grafted dextran/glycol-polymeric hydrogel (Dex/PNIPAM) for thermo-response triggered delivery of buprenorphine. The as-synthesized composite of GQDs and Dex/PNIPAM exhibited excellent biocompatibility and no harm of the surrounding tissues was observed. The developed hydrogel could be useful for thermo responsive drug delivery for pain management in tissue implantation. In another work, a pH-responsive GQD based system was developed for the delivery of antitumor drug doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) [123]. Fig. 3b presents the synthesis of PEGMA@GQDs@γ-CD-MOF composite. They designed the γ-cyclodextrin-based metal-organic framework (γ-CD-MOF) and then GQDs were embedded on it. Later, cancer cell-targeted probe was developed denoted as AS1411@PEGMA@GQDs@γ-CD-MOF. This probe exhibited responsive release function and outstanding targeting ability. Here the role of GQDs are to act as a fluorescent probe to track the distribution of drug delivery system at the sub-cellular level. The γ-CD-MOF and GQDs@γ-CD-MOF were found to be biocompatible. However, when the drug was loaded with it the cytotoxicity level was increased. The nanocomposite showed excellent solubility, biocompatibility, drug loading capacity, and excellent targeting ability, which make it unique in every aspect. More recently, Sinha et al. [124] reported a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based experimental analysis to understand the dynamics of the delivery of daunomycin (DN), widely used as a chemotherapeutic medication, by GQD. They have provided a detailed explanation of the possible route from the carrier to the targeted acceptor. Xue et al. [125] investigated the structure, thermodynamics, and dynamic properties of model drugs which are translocating into a POPC (phosphatidylcholine) lipid membrane with the assistance of GQDs. MD (Molecular dynamics) simulation and PMF (probability mass function) calculations revealed that GQDs can effectively bind with model drugs and bring it to the targeted molecule on the nanosecond timescale. The drug delivery process is facilitated as the precise size of GQDs minimizes the translocation free energy for the permeation into the membrane.

Excellent PL properties, good water solubility, and biocompatibility of GQDs make a promising candidate for the application in bioimaging, both in vivo and in vitro [126,127]. In addition, GQDs are easily permeable through the cell membrane due to its small size. Renal clearance rate of GQDs is also very fast because of fast biodegradation rate for having small sizes.

The use of GQDs as fluorophores for cell imaging has several advantages over conventional organic fluorophores and semiconductor QDs. To avoid toxic effect manifested by semiconductor fluorophores, GQDs have been widely used for cell imaging. Rajender et al. [128] synthesized solvent dependent and edge controlled GQDs. The high fluorescent yield of the GQDs was utilized to study the bio-imaging of cancer cells. Bright blue PL emission was observed inside the cancer cell lines. High biocompatibility of GQDs was observed with A-375 ​cells as compared to HeLa cells. Nasrollahi et al. [129] used GQDs as dual-functional targeted drug carriers and cellular bioimaging agents in their work. Most recently, Hasan et al. [130] showed that rare earth metal ions Nd3+ and Tm3+-doped GQDs can be used for NIR in vivo and in vitro imaging. Bright field/NIR overlay images and normalized fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells in the presence of different composites of GQDs have been shown in the Fig. 4(a-c) and 4 (d-e), respectively. They conjugated GQDs with a variable fragment of an antibody (scFv) and modified to GQDsscFv10 and this had drug loading capacity of around 50% on the surface with pH-dependent release of drugs cisplatin at the neutral condition. GQDs-scFvB10 was incubated with MDA-MB-231 ​cells and visualized through confocal laser microscopy. The strong green fluorescent GQDs-scFvB10 was located in the cytoplasm and nucleus of cells, which was confirmed from the confocal fluorescence images of the cells. Geng et al. [131] used N-GQDs to label cells in the absence of organic dyes, utilizing the small size and good cell permeability of it. N-GQDs were homogeneously distributed without aggregation in the cytoplasm. Roy et al. [132]utilized the plant-leaf-derived GQDs modified to AbA5-GQDs for labeling apoptotic cells. Wang and co-workers [133] reported biomass-derived N-GQDs for multicolor cell imaging. Fibroblast cells were incubated in an aqueous solution of N-GQDs and multi-colored PL (blue, green, and yellow) inside the cells were observed.

GQDs are promising carbon nanomaterials in biological applications due to their excellent photoluminescent properties. Hence, GQDs can be utilized for in-vivo imaging [134]. Wang et al. [135] showed that GQDs can effectively inhibit in-vivo aggregation and thereby reduces toxicity of human islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP). The strong interactions between GQDs and IAPP through electrostatic, hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding help eliminate the toxic intermediates. The in-vivo experiment was carried out using zebrafish embryos. The effect of microinjection of IAPP in zebrafish embryos has been presented in Figure 5 (a-c). They found that strong interaction between GQDs and IAPP quenches the nanostructure autofluorescence and reduces the β-sheet content in the peptide. High affinity at psychological pH boosted the GQD-IAPP association in the embryonic environment. Excellent binding between GQDs and IAPP prohibited in-vivo toxicity. Inhibition in peptide aggregation and toxicity make GQD a promising candidate for the application as nanomedicine. In a recent study, GQDs were loaded into optical-magneto ferroferricoxide@polypyrrole (Fe3O4@PPy) core-shell nanoparticles [136]. The study focuses on the development of an efficient and convenient one-pot method to load GQDs into nanoparticles and its subsequent application as a fluorophore for in-vivo imaging. Mao et al. [137] developed a bionanosensor based on rN-GQDs and CoOOH composite. They employed this biosensor for sensing cerebral ascorbic acid in rat brain microdialysate. Ge et al. [138] reported a new photodynamic therapy (PDT) agent based on GQDs. The broad absorption, deep red emission, and high singlet oxygen generation yield of GQDs could be utilized for in vivo imaging and cancer therapy. To investigate the fluorescence imaging potential of GQDs, they injected an aqueous GQDs solution in the back of a mouse. A much higher fluorescent intensity was observed on the injecting site. No fluorescence intensity decay was observed even after one week of injection in the injecting site. After injecting GQDs (dose: 4 ​mg/kg) and irradiating twice, the effect on the tumor was observed. The tumor was decomposed within 17 days of injection and no further regrowth of the tumor was observed. Hence, GQDs can be employed successfully in medical applications and in-vivo fluorescence imaging.

GQDs have been widely used as biosensor as it exhibits excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. GQDs can detect biomolecules, ions, and other compounds with high selectivity and sensitivity [139]. The high quantum yield and tunable fluorescence of GQDs are advantages for sensing. Kalkal et al. [140] developed amine functionalized GQDs based fluorescent biosensor for the detection of lung cancer cell biomarker via fluorescence ‘turn on’ mechanism, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. Qian et al. [141] reported a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based platform for the sensing of DNA using GQDs. The system can effectively identify complementary and mismatched nucleic acid sequences with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. The system can be applied also for in vivo and in vitro imaging, and promote the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in immunoassays. Kermani et al. [142] used biocompitable GQDs as fluorescence probe for the DNA methylation detection and DNA MTase activity assay. They also investigated detection of MTase activity by fluorescence anisotropy. The DNA probe was connected with the GQDs through end that contains CCGG sequence. The quenching of fluorescent intensity of GQDs was observed due to the interaction of GQDs to DNA strands. However, fluorescence intensity was found to be enhanced when HpII cleaved DNA strands due to insufficient M.SssI enzyme. Song Wang and coworkers [143] designed GQDs-aptamer-cDNA bioconjugates for the detection of ochratoxin A (OTA). The detection strategy uses the advantage of structure switching signaling of aptamer on combining with GQDs. Quenching mechanism based on aggregation and disaggregation of GQDs in the presence of DNA aptamer has been presented on Fig. 6b. The fluorescence signal-on based platform can detect OTA with high selectivity and sensitivity. A detection limit of 13 ​pg/mL was observed with 95–103% recovery in red wine samples. Recently, Cheng et al. [144] reported selective sensing of lysine using GQDs. Lysine was adsorbed on the GQDs surface via electrostatic interactions. At 420 ​nm the fluorescence enhancement was observed. Because of having a small diameter, lysine approaches GQDs easily, which is responsible for the change of GQDs. N-GQDs and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs(N,S-GQDs) show higher sensitivity than GQDs. Soleymani et al. [145] reported detection of cancer cell through biorecepter conjugated N-GQDs. Functionalized sensing of cancer cells using folate bioreceptor functionalized N-GQDs. A comparison was made to check the specificity of the folate decorated Nitrogen-doped GQDs (FA-N-GQDs) nanocomposite towards the detection of MKN 45 with HEK 293 as a normal cell. A minimum amount of attachment was observed when GQDs were allowed to make contact with MKN 45. The targeting ability of GQDs toward the cancer cells was confirmed by these measurements. Ju et al. [146] synthesized N-GQDs through one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and dicyandiamide. The quantum yield of as-synthesized N-GQDs is around 32.4%. With this high quantum yield, it was applied as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of glutathione. Photoluminescence intensity of N-GQDs was quenched by adding Hg (II). The PL was recovered by using GSH due to the combination of GSH-Hg (II) interaction. Mars et al. [147]developed a Curcumin-GQDs based dual-mode biosensor for sensitive detection of APOe4, responsible for Alzheimer's disease. They utilized the fluorescence and electrochemical properties of curcumin electropolymerized on GQDs-ITO (Indium–Tin-Oxide) surface. The dual-mode sensing platform exhibited good reproducibility, sensitivity, and selectivity. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor was found to be 0.48 ​pg ​mL−1. Wang and co-workers [148] reported GQDs-enzyme based biosensor for the determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose. Hydrogen peroxide was formed by the oxidation of glucose using glucose oxidase (GOx). The produced hydrogen peroxide was later allowed to oxidize phenol to quinone. Due to oxidation of phenol by hydrogen peroxide, the fluorescence of GQDs–GOx–HRP–phenol system was quenched. LOD for glucose was 0.08 μ mol/L.

Because of unique properties, GQDs find applications in emerging biomedical, optical and energy related areas. GQDs synthesized through different approaches like top-down and bottom-up have some advantages and drawbacks. Microwave-assisted synthesis is simple and one of the fastest methods. The acidic exfoliation methods used been extensively employed for the synthesis of good quality GQDs, although the methods are cumbersome and time-consuming. Thus, the synthesizing of GQDs with acid exfoliation method with a simple procedure is still challenging and needs to be explored. Purification of GQDs may be done shortly by utilizing size-selective precipitation method instead of dialyzing for days. GQDs can easily get functionalized with functional groups by using different techniques. The synthetic procedure may be chosen based on applications. The applications of GQDs are diverse. In very recent years, GQDs with various edge functionalization have been gaining tremendous prospects in the field of drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing due to their small size, high fluorescence, and low toxicity. Excellent loading capacity of GQDs is a key advantage in drug delivery.

However, some of the properties of GQDs and mechanism of interactions with different biological systems require more investigation for greater understanding of the functional aspects. Reports on red-emitting GQDs are scarce. Red emitting GQDs can be of great significance in bioimaging applications because of the low absorption in tissues in the range. In the future, research needs to be carried out on developing more sensitive and selective biosensors based on the GQDs. In-vivo toxicity of GQDs are very low. Hence, more investigation on the toxicological aspects of GQDs for long term exposure will help understand its potential for biological applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors (S Karmakar and S. Maiti) wish to thank DST, Government of India for providing INSPIRE fellowship. Two of the authors (T.K. Das and S. Kundu) are thankful to the CSIR and UGC, for NET fellowship.
